step_by_step_thinking:
Staphylococcus albus is rare and not mentioned specifically in any context related to endocarditis. Streptococcus faecalis (an enterococcus) is listed in several sources as causing endocarditis (e.g., "enterococci" in general, and specific mention of S. faecalis linked to IE). Salmonella typhi is referenced in relation to discitis ("Salmonella spp.") and mentions of salmonellae in association with septic emboli, though not explicitly stated as a primary cause of endocarditis. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is directly cited as capable of causing endocarditis in the evidence (specifically “Pseudomonas aeruginosa...endocarditis”).
Therefore, while S. albus is not described or linked to endocarditis in any part of the document—whereas the others are discussed—even indirectly—the absence of any reference makes it statistically and clinically unlikely compared to the others which do have documented associations. Hence, it would be least likely to cause infective endocarditis according to the given data.
